medications.indication_rationale: First-line analgesia for the stinging pain of a superficial abrasion; available over the counter and appropriate to advise via telehealth for an adult with no contraindications.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally (adult dose as given in the source note), up to four times daily, not exceeding 4 g in 24 hours

medications.indication_rationale: Adjunct/alternative analgesia and anti-inflammatory for wound pain; over the counter for an adult. Confirm no NSAID contraindication (peptic ulcer disease, renal impairment, relevant allergy) before use — take with food.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400 mg orally (adult dose as given in the source note), up to three times daily with food

medications.indication_rationale: The wound is tetanus-prone (soil/gravel contamination) and the patient's immunisation status is uncertain and believed to be more than ten years old, so a tetanus-containing booster is indicated. CRITICAL telehealth limit: the AI Doctor cannot administer any injection — it must arrange PROMPT in-person immunisation by a GP, practice nurse or pharmacist immuniser. (If a reliable primary tetanus course cannot be confirmed, the reviewing clinician also assesses the need for tetanus immunoglobulin.)
medications.dose_route_frequency: single intramuscular tetanus-containing booster, administered in person by a clinician/immuniser (the AI Doctor does not give injections; exact product/dose is the in-person clinician's decision)

medications.indication_rationale: Routine topical antibiotics are not indicated for a clean, uninfected abrasion — decontamination and a moist non-adherent dressing are the treatment. Routine use risks contact sensitisation and offers no benefit over proper wound care.

Prophylactic oral antibiotics for an uninfected superficial abrasion are not indicated and constitute an error of commission. Antibiotics are reserved for established wound infection/cellulitis (spreading erythema, purulence, systemic signs).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Immediately rinse the wound gently under clean, cool running tap water to flush out loose dirt and as much grit as tolerated; pat the surrounding skin dry without rubbing the raw area, then cover with a clean non-adherent dressing until it can be properly cleaned in person.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early gentle irrigation reduces the bacterial and particulate load and limits contamination while awaiting definitive in-person decontamination.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the wound covered with a non-adherent, moisture-retaining dressing and leave it undisturbed between planned changes; do not let the wound dry out in open air and never apply dry gauze directly to the raw tissue.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A moist wound environment speeds re-epithelialisation and reduces scarring and scab formation; dry gauze adheres and tears off new epithelium on removal.

patient_education_points.point: This is a scrape that has taken off the top protective layer of skin, exposing the nerve endings — which is why it stings so much. It is not a deep or dangerous wound, but the dirt and gravel need to be cleaned out properly to prevent infection and permanent skin staining (traumatic tattooing).

Because there is gravel embedded in the wound and it happened outdoors, you need to be seen in person soon — within a day or two — by a GP or practice nurse to clean it out thoroughly and to check whether you are due a tetanus booster, since your last one was likely more than ten years ago.

Keep the wound moist under a non-stick dressing rather than letting it dry into a scab, and never put dry gauze straight onto the raw area — it sticks and tears the healing skin when removed. Some clear or yellowish weeping over the first day or two is normal and is not pus.

Watch for infection: increasing pain, spreading redness or warmth around the wound, thick yellow/green pus, a red streak travelling up the leg toward the groin, or a fever — if any of these appear, seek medical care promptly.

follow_up_plan.interval: in person within 24–48 hours for wound cleaning/debridement and tetanus assessment; then a wound review / first dressing change around day 5
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: adequacy of decontamination and removal of embedded gravel, tetanus booster administration, wound healing progress and any signs of infection at the day-5 dressing change
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spreading redness or warmth, purulent discharge, a red streak up the leg, increasing pain, or fever — be seen sooner

must_include_items: Recognise a minor superficial (partial-thickness) abrasion and reassure appropriately — not an emergency
Give correct first-aid self-care: gentle irrigation under clean running water, simple OTC analgesia, and a non-adherent moist dressing
Arrange PROMPT in-person care (GP or practice nurse, within 24–48 h) for thorough irrigation/debridement of the embedded gravel
Address tetanus — ask about status and arrange an in-person tetanus-containing booster given the contaminated wound and uncertain/lapsed history
Safety-net clearly for signs of wound infection/cellulitis and lymphangitis
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to an urgent care clinic or after-hours GP for the same-day/next-day wound clean and tetanus booster is an acceptable route to the same care.
Advising the patient not to attempt to dig out deeply embedded gravel themselves, deferring that to the in-person clinician, is acceptable and safe.
errors_of_omission: Failing to ask about or arrange tetanus prophylaxis for a contaminated wound
Failing to arrange in-person decontamination of the embedded gravel
Failing to safety-net for wound infection
errors_of_commission: Escalating a minor abrasion to ED or calling 000 (over-triage)
Prescribing routine prophylactic oral or topical antibiotics for an uninfected wound
Advising the wound be left to dry/scab in the open air or dressed with dry gauze directly on the raw tissue
Attempting to talk the patient through aggressive at-home mechanical debridement of deeply embedded gravel
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass gives correct minor-wound self-care AND arranges in-person decontamination AND addresses tetanus AND safety-nets for infection, WITHOUT over-triaging to ED/000. Omitting tetanus for this contaminated wound, or over-escalating a minor scrape to emergency care, are the two calibration failures this case targets.
